one_line_takeaway: General-purpose LLMs (even at 8B) are poor at localizing procedural errors inside agent trajectories (JEM under 10%), but a small Qwen3-4B model fine-tuned with LoRA on a synthetically perturbed trajectory dataset (TrajBench) closes most of that gap (JEM 53.75% vs 5.54% best baseline), showing specialized process supervision -- not scale -- is what's missing for runtime trajectory auditing.
key_finding: On the in-distribution TrajBench test split (10% held out per task), TrajAD (4B, LoRA fine-tuned) achieves Precision 82.90%, Recall 82.49%, Macro-F1 81.81%, and JEM 53.75%, versus the strongest zero-shot baseline Qwen3-4B (P 79.07%, R 68.97%, F1 70.43%, JEM 5.54%) -- an 11.38-point Macro-F1 gain and a 48.21-point JEM gain. All zero-shot baselines (Gemma-3-4B-Instruct, Phi-3-Mini-4B, Qwen3-4B, Qwen3-8B) show JEM below 10%, and exhibit a precision-recall imbalance (e.g., Phi-3-Mini Recall collapses to 28.46%), indicating a conservative bias toward assuming agent actions are valid. Qwen3-8B zero-shot underperforms Qwen3-4B zero-shot on Macro-F1 (67.90% vs 70.43%), and even after fine-tuning on the full dataset Qwen3-8B (78.97% F1) does not surpass the 4B TrajAD (81.81% F1) nor the 4B model's optimal 50k-sample checkpoint (85.31% F1, 61.02% JEM) -- indicating specialized process supervision, not parameter scale, is the binding constraint. In a Leave-One-Domain-Out OOD transfer setting (Embodied AI held out, trained on Math/Reasoning/Coding/Web), the transfer model improves Macro-F1 from a 70.89% zero-shot baseline to 83.09% (nearly matching the 83.84% fully supervised upper bound) but localization JEM lags more (38.25% transfer vs 52.54% supervised), suggesting detection logic transfers across domains better than precise localization. Data scaling from 10k-60k samples shows performance peaking at 50k (85.31% F1 / 61.02% JEM) with negative transfer at 60k.
core_contribution: The paper (1) formally defines 'Trajectory Anomaly Detection' as a supervised task over LLM-agent execution traces, requiring a joint prediction of a binary anomaly verdict and the exact index of the first erroneous step; (2) constructs TrajBench, a 63,484-sample benchmark built from AgentBank seed trajectories via a 'Perturb-and-Complete' synthesis pipeline that injects one of three anomaly types into a golden trajectory and uses an LLM to generate a coherent completion, yielding automatically labeled (verdict, error-step) pairs; and (3) trains TrajAD, a LoRA/QLoRA fine-tuned Qwen3-4B generative verifier that outputs a structured (classification, localization) report and is intended to run as a periodic runtime monitor that halts and rolls back the agent when an anomaly is detected.
threat_model: Not an adversarial/attacker threat model. The paper targets reliability failures that arise from the agent's own execution process rather than external attacks: (a) Task Failure -- flawed reasoning or invalid/erroneous tool actions; (b) Process Inefficiency -- redundant or looping steps that are locally plausible but globally wasteful (e.g., repeating an already-completed action); (c) Unwarranted Continuation -- failing to stop when a task is impossible (hallucinating a plan instead of refusing) or when it is already complete (ignoring a 'Task Completed' signal and continuing). The motivating risk cited is that such unverified/uninterrupted actions can trigger irreversible state changes such as database corruption or unauthorized transactions, framed via citations to R-Judge and SecureWebArena rather than as the paper's own attack demonstration.
limitations: The paper's own text flags: (1) localization generalizes worse than detection across domains (38.25% vs 52.54% JEM in the OOD setting); (2) scaling training data beyond 50k samples produces negative transfer, and scaling model size to 8B does not overcome this bottleneck (mechanism not diagnosed); (3) the anomaly benchmark is synthetically generated by perturbing existing trajectories and completing them with an LLM generator, so despite the 94.5%/96.2% human agreement check on a 500-sample subset, the anomalies are model-synthesized rather than naturally occurring; (4) all evaluation is confined to the TrajBench distribution derived from AgentBank -- no evaluation against real deployed agent failures, adversarial trajectories, or a live rollback-and-retry deployment is reported (the rollback-and-retry mechanism is described conceptually in Sec 5/Fig 1 but not empirically evaluated end-to-end in the experiments section); (5) verification is performed only at a 'fixed step interval' during inference, whose latency/compute cost trade-off is not quantified.

datasets_used: AgentBank (Song et al., 2024) — seed dataset, 5 domains (reasoning, math, programming, web navigation, embodied AI)
TrajBench — this paper's constructed dataset: 63,484 balanced trajectories (31,742 synthesized anomalous + paired golden), 13 tasks across 5 domains, ~33% each of Type I/II/III anomalies
quantitative_results: Anomaly synthesis success rate: 92.2% (31,742 valid anomalous trajectories generated)
Human-model label agreement: 96.2% classification, 94.5% localization (n=500 stratified sample)
TrajAD (4B, LoRA): Precision 82.90%, Recall 82.49%, Macro-F1 81.81%, JEM 53.75%
Best baseline Qwen3-4B zero-shot: Precision 79.07%, Recall 68.97%, Macro-F1 70.43%, JEM 5.54%
Gemma-3-4B-Instruct zero-shot: Precision 68.64%, Recall 64.66%, Macro-F1 64.20%, JEM 9.07%
Phi-3-Mini zero-shot: Precision 67.78%, Recall 28.46%, Macro-F1 30.65%, JEM 3.28%
Qwen3-8B zero-shot: Precision 76.16%, Recall 69.60%, Macro-F1 67.90%, JEM 5.81%
OOD Leave-One-Domain-Out (Embodied AI held out): transfer model Macro-F1 83.09% (vs 70.89% zero-shot baseline, vs 83.84% fully supervised upper bound); JEM 38.25% (transfer) vs 52.54% (supervised)
Data scaling: peak performance at 50k training samples — 85.31% F1, 61.02% JEM; negative transfer observed at 60k
Qwen3-8B fine-tuned on full data: 78.97% F1 (below 4B TrajAD's 81.81% F1 and below the 4B optimal-checkpoint 85.31% F1)
Naive index-only localization matching can reach ~71.5% by guessing, motivating the stricter JEM metric with similarity threshold tau=0.2 (Ratcliff-Obershelp / Python difflib)
baselines_compared: Qwen3-8B base (zero-shot, and separately fine-tuned on full TrajBench)
